title: Tensile deformation behavior analysis of low density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels
abstract_summary: Three low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with carbon contents of 0.5, 0.8, and 1.2 wt% were investigated to correlate microstructures with tensile strain hardening behavior. The 0.5C steel exhibited duplex ferrite–austenite, the 0.8C steel contained ordered phases in both phases, and the 1.2C steel was fully austenitic with κ-carbides. Crussard–Jaoul analysis revealed two-stage hardening for 0.5C and 0.8C steels and three-stage hardening for 1.2C steel, attributed to distinct dislocation substructure evolutions.
research_objective: To clarify the differences in strain hardening behavior among Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with varying carbon contents by correlating microstructural phase constituents and deformation-induced dislocation structures using Crussard–Jaoul analysis.
main_process_variables: Hot rolling at 1200 °C after homogenization for 3h to 3.5 mm thickness (90% reduction)
Final annealing at 1000 °C followed by water quenching
Tensile testing at room temperature with initial strain rate 1.33×10^{-3} s^{-1}
key_sections: Introduction: background on low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels and motivation for studying strain hardening
Experimental: alloy preparation, thermomechanical processing, tension tests, optical and TEM microscopy
Results: pre-deformation microstructures, tensile properties and flow curves, microstructural evolution during deformation
Discussion: influence of microstructure on mechanical properties and on strain hardening behavior
Conclusions: summary linking carbon content, phase constituents, and multi-stage strain hardening
